The future of radiology: The path towards multimodal AI and superdiagnostics

Abstract
--------

The transformative power of artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping radiology, medicine, and healthcare, marking radiology as a pioneering specialty in AI adoption. The digital nature of radiological data and standardized data formats positioned radiology as the ideal testing ground for clinical AI integration. While initial enthusiasm led to inflated expectations, fueled by linear thinking and the planning fallacy, AI has now matured into tools that augment, rather than replace, radiologists’ expertise. Radiologists’ role is evolving from image interpreters to diagnostic orchestrators in a multimodal era. The integration of imaging data with diverse sources such as genomics, pathology, and wearable sensors necessitates a shift to a systems-level perspective. This transformation demands not only technical literacy but also interdisciplinary collaboration to effectively synthesize AI-driven insights and mitigate cognitive overload. Radiologists must navigate uncertainty, adopt structured workflows, and communicate AI-supported findings clearly to maintain trust in diagnostics. The emergence of generative AI, particularly large language models, further streamlines AI adoption by enabling intuitive, human-centered interfaces. However, addressing the growing knowledge gap is crucial. Traditional radiology training must be overhauled to incorporate data science, bioinformatics, and systems biology, ensuring radiologists are prepared to lead multimodal diagnostics. Radiologists are uniquely positioned to spearhead this transition, leveraging AI to integrate diverse data streams, improve patient care, and foster collaboration across specialties. Proactive adaptation will secure radiologists’ central role in AI-driven medicine, safeguarding the human element in healthcare while advancing diagnostic precision.

Introduction
------------

> “Twenty-five years ago, the World Wide Web was just getting off the ground. Therefore, when thinking of the medical research landscape in 25 years, it is reasonable to think big and without limits.” – Eric Topol [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib1).

This quote from Eric Topol captures the essence of the transformative period we find ourselves in today. The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping the field of radiology, medicine and society as a whole. In medicine, Radiology, as a digital-first specialty, has become a testing ground for AI applications in medicine and is positioned as a leader in the adoption of AI-driven technologies. The question we now face is not whether AI will play a role in the future of radiology, but how radiologists will adapt and lead this change and whether they are even willing to seize this historic opportunity to reshape healthcare for the better.

The early adoption of AI in radiology
-------------------------------------

Radiology was one of the first medical specialties to embrace AI, and this was no coincidence. The digital nature of radiological data, standardized formats such as DICOM [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib2), and the early adoption of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) following the “ImageNet moment” [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib3) provided a fertile ground for AI integration. CNNs, designed for image processing, were naturally suited to radiological applications, making radiology an ideal field for initial AI deployment. However, the initial excitement surrounding AI’s potential quickly led to inflated expectations. Predictions that AI would soon replace radiologists, including a high-profile statement [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib4) from pioneer and now Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton, reflected a common cognitive bias: the tendency to think linearly in an exponential world.

The pitfalls of linear thinking in an exponential age
-----------------------------------------------------

Humans are prone to linear thinking, often underestimating the phases of exponential growth while overestimating short-term progress [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib5). This misjudgment is further aggravated by the _planning fallacy_, a concept introduced by Kahneman and Tversky [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib6). The planning fallacy describes our tendency to underestimate the time, costs, and risks associated with future tasks, even when we have historical data that suggests otherwise. In the context of AI development, many experts and stakeholders anticipated a rapid deployment of AI solutions in clinical practice, assuming a linear trajectory of progress based on early successes in research environments. This fallacy led to overly optimistic projections about the timeline for AI adoption and fueled unrealistic expectations about its capabilities, particularly in complex, real-world clinical scenarios.

This cognitive bias is evident in the adoption curve of many new technologies, as described by the Gartner Hype Cycle [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib7). In the case of AI in radiology, the initial wave of enthusiasm gave way to disillusionment as it became clear that early AI models, while promising, were not ready to fully replace human expertise.

Radiology has now moved beyond this initial hype phase. We are witnessing a steady, incremental integration of AI tools that assist rather than replace radiologists, focusing on enhancing productivity and reducing cognitive load [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib8). AI is being used to automate routine, mundane tasks, allowing radiologists to concentrate on more complex, intellectually demanding cases. However, recognizing the influence of the planning fallacy on our past assumptions should encourage a more tempered and realistic approach as we continue to integrate AI into medical practice. By acknowledging the challenges of scaling technology in clinical settings, we can better manage expectations and develop strategies that align with the gradual, iterative nature of true innovation.

Radiologists as orchestrators in a multimodal era
-------------------------------------------------

As Jha and Topol noted in their seminal JAMA article [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib9), the role of the radiologist is evolving from a traditional image interpreter to an information specialist. This shift is not merely a cosmetic change but a fundamental transformation driven by the increasing complexity and volume of diagnostic data. Radiologists have always relied on additional clinical information - such as laboratory tests, clinical notes, and pathology results - because interpreting imaging findings in the context of the overall clinical picture leads to more accurate and meaningful diagnoses.

The same principle applies to AI models. There is a growing trend toward multimodal AI systems that integrate imaging data with other non-imaging inputs. These multimodal approaches have the potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy and clinical utility, mirroring how radiologists synthesize information today. By combining diverse data sources, such models are expected to dominate the field in the future, driving a new era of precision diagnostics and improved patient care.

The growing availability of data from sources such as molecular diagnostics, genomic profiles, electronic health records, and wearable sensors - while undeniably making multimodal AI more powerful - also introduces new challenges for radiologists. This marks a fundamental shift in how radiologists approach diagnostics. Traditionally, diagnosis has been a largely linear process centered around the interpretation of a single data stream, primarily imaging, complemented by basic clinical information. In the multimodal era, however, this process is becoming increasingly non-linear, requiring the simultaneous synthesis of multiple, often disparate, data sources.

To navigate this complexity, radiologists must adopt a more holistic, systems-level perspective. Their role is evolving into that of a diagnostic orchestrator, coordinating, validating or rejecting, weighing, and synthesizing AI-generated insights drawn from a vast and varied array of information streams. This transformation not only demands a shift in mindset but also new skills to ensure that multimodal AI serves as an enabler of better patient care rather than an additional layer of complexity.

The success of this transformation relies on the establishment of efficient human-machine teaming, as emphasized by Henry et al. [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib10). Rather than replacing clinical judgment, AI systems must be designed to complement and enhance diagnostic and treatment management processes. Acting as collaborative partners, these tools serve as a _second pair of eyes_, assisting radiologists in identifying potential issues and alleviating cognitive burden. This allows radiologists to focus on the most critical and complex aspects of patient care. By fostering this synergistic relationship, AI not only enhances decision-making but also preserves and amplifies radiologists’ expertise, driving a more efficient and accurate diagnostic process.

The advent of generative AI, particularly large language models (LLMs), represents a paradigm shift in human-machine interaction [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib11). Traditionally, healthcare providers had to adapt to the rigid, prescriptive nature of machines, tailoring their inputs to accommodate technological constraints. With LLMs, this dynamic is fundamentally reversed: machines now adapt to human communication, understanding natural language and responding intuitively in a conversational manner. This revolution in interaction simplifies the integration of AI into daily clinical workflows, enabling radiologists to seamlessly access, interpret, and act on complex, multimodal insights. Ultimately, this enhances their capacity to deliver more accurate and high-quality patient care.

However, this shift also introduces significant cognitive and practical challenges. The human brain is inherently limited in its ability to process vast and varied streams of information simultaneously [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib14). While experienced radiologists excel at contextualizing imaging findings through years of clinical expertise, the integration of increasingly complex, non-visual data—such as whole-genome sequencing results or proteomic analyses—risks overwhelming their cognitive capacity. If not carefully managed, we may merely replace information overload on a lower data level with a new kind of overload at the results and interpretation level.

To address this, workflows must be thoughtfully designed to align with the human brain’s inherent limitations in parallel processing. AI tools and systems should be tailored to filter, prioritize, and present information in a way that enhances human strengths - such as moral reasoning, ethical judgment, oversight, self-correction, and adaptability - rather than competing with them. By supporting radiologists as decision-makers rather than inundating them with unmanageable insights, we can ensure that AI augments clinical expertise without compromising efficiency or accuracy. This approach will foster a more balanced, effective, and sustainable integration of multimodal AI into diagnostic workflows.

Furthermore, the evolving role of radiologists as information specialists brings up an important issue of legitimacy and trust. As AI systems become more sophisticated and their recommendations more opaque, there is a growing need for radiologists to act as intermediaries between AI outputs and clinical decisions. This role requires not only technical expertise but also strong communication skills to effectively convey complex, data-driven insights to clinicians and patients. Without this interpretative layer, there is a risk that the diagnostic process could become a _black box_, undermining the trust that patients place in the medical system and clinicians place in radiologists [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib16). Radiologists must therefore rise to the challenge, embracing their expanded role as the linchpins of a data-rich, AI-enhanced diagnostic landscape.

However, fully embracing the role of an information specialist requires radiologists also to strike a delicate balance between acknowledging uncertainty and providing decisive, evidence-based guidance. Radiologists have traditionally managed diagnostic uncertainty by offering broad differential diagnoses, adopting a purely descriptive approach in their reports or referring to the limitations of image quality or the sufficiency of available data. This cautious approach stems from the weight of their responsibility; a single misinterpretation can lead to serious consequences, potentially impacting a patient’s treatment and even determining life or death outcomes. As radiologists take on a broader role in the diagnostic process, integrating diverse data sources and guiding clinical decisions with multimodal AI, the magnitude of their responsibility increases, amplifying the inherent uncertainty. This amplified uncertainty can potentially lead to diagnostic hesitancy, where radiologists may become even more cautious in their interpretations or rely excessively on caveats and qualifications. Such an approach, while understandable given the stakes, may inadvertently reduce the clarity and actionable value of their diagnostic reports. To address this issue, it is essential to foster a culture of shared responsibility and collaborative decision-making in clinical practice. By integrating multidisciplinary teams and leveraging the collective expertise of specialists from various fields, radiologists can mitigate the burden of individual uncertainty. Regular case discussions, interdisciplinary consultations, and the use of boards or diagnostic conferences can help validate complex interpretations, providing a safety net for radiologists as they navigate the complexities of multimodal data.

Bridging the knowledge gap: from image interpreters to multimodal information specialists
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The rise of multimodal AI in radiology will mark a significant shift in the diagnostic landscape, driven by the superior performance of these models compared to traditional unimodal approaches [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib17). These sophisticated AI systems can integrate diverse data inputs, ranging from imaging and lab results to complex genomic profiles and histopathological analyses. However, the effective supervision and utilization of such multimodal tools demand a profound understanding not necessarily of the AI algorithms themselves but of the diverse types of medical data they process.

This transformation raises critical questions about the qualifications and preparedness of radiologists for this new role. Traditional radiology training focuses heavily on mastering the nuances of image interpretation and understanding the physics behind various imaging modalities, such as MRI and CT. While these skills remain essential, they are no longer sufficient in an era where diagnostic decisions must incorporate inputs from a broad spectrum of medical data, including complex non-imaging information such as genomic sequences, proteomic analyses, and advanced immunological markers. The challenge is that these data types often fall outside the typical expertise of a radiologist, creating a significant knowledge gap.

This gap is exacerbated by the rapid development of multimodal AI models that are capable of processing and analyzing not only images but also text, lab results, and time-series data from wearable biosensors or electrocardiograms. These models have the potential to provide unprecedented insights, but they also introduce new layers of complexity. For example, a multimodal AI system might integrate imaging findings with genomic data to suggest a targeted therapy or flag a potential genetic predisposition. However, without a solid understanding of the underlying genomics or the context of these findings, radiologists may struggle to interpret the results effectively or assess the validity of the AI’s recommendations.

The current educational framework for radiologists does not adequately prepare them for this evolving landscape. Although radiologists are trained in general medicine during their years at medical school, the focus of their specialty training narrows significantly to image analysis and interpretation. The rapid pace of advancements in fields like genomics, molecular pathology or systems biology has far outstripped the traditional medical curriculum, leading to a situation where many practicing radiologists may not have the up-to-date knowledge necessary to fully leverage or critically evaluate these new multimodal AI tools. Moreover, the half-life of medical knowledge is shrinking, meaning that what was once cutting-edge information during medical school may now be outdated or even obsolete. This presents a significant challenge for radiologists who must continuously update their skills and knowledge to keep pace, not even mentioning innovations in AI and data science. As a result, there is a pressing need to reevaluate and expand the educational framework for radiologists, equipping them with both the interdisciplinary medical expertise and the technical literacy required to effectively oversee and integrate multimodal AI into clinical practice.

The traditional curriculum, focused primarily on image interpretation and modality-specific expertise, is no longer sufficient in an era of multimodal AI. A comprehensive overhaul is needed - one that integrates foundational courses in data science, bioinformatics, and systems biology with core radiology training. This expanded curriculum should equip radiologists with the technical literacy to understand the principles behind AI algorithms, critically assess their outputs, and incorporate these insights seamlessly into clinical decision-making.

A key element of this transformation is the inclusion of interdisciplinary training. Radiologists must go beyond their traditional focus and develop expertise that intersects with genomics, pathology, oncology, laboratory medicine, immunology, molecular biology, computational medicine, and many more. By collaborating closely with specialists in these fields, radiologists can gain a deeper understanding of the diverse data inputs that modern AI systems analyze, enabling them to effectively interpret complex, multimodal outputs. This shift towards an interdisciplinary approach is essential to bridge the current knowledge gap and to ensure that radiologists are not merely passive users of AI but active participants in the development and refinement of these tools.

Additionally, radiology training programs must adapt to prepare future radiologists for this expanded role. Emphasis should be placed not only on medical knowledge, technical skills, and AI literacy but also on building confidence in decision-making under uncertainty. Drawing from Tversky and Kahneman’s research [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib5), we recognize that radiologists - as all humans - are prone to cognitive biases, such as overconfidence in heuristic-based judgments or the _illusion of validity_, where they may rely excessively on pattern recognition without fully accounting for statistical uncertainties. Therefore, training should include risk assessment and probabilistic reasoning to counteract these biases and improve the accuracy of clinical judgments. Developing skills in effective communication of probabilistic findings and structured reporting can help radiologists articulate their conclusions clearly, even when faced with ambiguous or conflicting data [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib18), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S305057712500012X#bib19).

However, realizing this vision requires more than an update to the curriculum - it necessitates a significant cultural shift within the field of radiology. Proactive planning and strategic foresight are needed to redefine the role of radiologists as _integrative information specialists_. This involves embracing continuous learning and fostering a mindset that values interdisciplinary collaboration. By embedding these principles into the educational framework, we can prepare radiologists to navigate the increasing complexity of multimodal data and collaborate effectively with bioinformaticians, geneticists, and other specialists - or ultimately merge various diagnostic disciplines into a new field of interdisciplinary clinical information specialists, maybe then referred to as _superdiagnosticians_.

Moreover, there is a pressing need to formalize the role of the radiologist as a leader in diagnostic integration. Radiologists are uniquely positioned to bridge the gap between diverse diagnostic domains, given their central role in patient care and their experience in synthesizing imaging data with clinical context. By embracing this expanded role, radiologists can leverage AI tools to integrate imaging findings with laboratory results, genomic data, and pathology reports, providing a holistic view of the patient’s condition. This shift towards a more integrative approach will not only enhance the diagnostic process but also solidify the role of radiology at the forefront of patient care.

The strategic role of radiology in leading AI adoption
------------------------------------------------------

Radiology is uniquely positioned to lead the charge towards _Superdiagnostics_, an era where diagnostic capabilities are enhanced by sophisticated, multimodal AI systems. Radiologists are already accustomed to synthesizing information from various sources and collaborating across specialties, making them ideal candidates to oversee the integration of these advanced tools. In fact, the groundwork for this leadership role has been laid over the past several years, as radiology was the first medical specialty to witness the clinical implementation of AI tools. Currently, the majority of regulatory approved AI applications in medicine are found in radiology, underscoring its role as the digital pioneer among medical disciplines.

Abstract
--------

Humanity stands at a pivotal moment of technological revolution, with artificial intelligence (AI) reshaping fields traditionally reliant on human cognitive abilities. This transition, driven by advancements in artificial neural networks, has transformed data processing and evaluation, creating opportunities for addressing complex and time-consuming tasks with AI solutions. Convolutional networks (CNNs) and the adoption of GPU technology have already revolutionized image recognition by enhancing computational efficiency and accuracy. In radiology, AI applications are particularly valuable for tasks involving pattern detection and classification; for example, AI tools have enhanced diagnostic accuracy and efficiency in detecting abnormalities across imaging modalities through automated feature extraction. Our analysis reveals that neuroimaging and chest imaging, as well as CT and MRI modalities, are the primary focus areas for AI products, reflecting their high clinical demand and complexity. AI tools are also used to target high-prevalence diseases, such as lung cancer, stroke, and breast cancer, underscoring AI’s alignment with impactful diagnostic needs. The regulatory landscape is a critical factor in AI product development, with the majority of products certified under the Medical Device Directive (MDD) and Medical Device Regulation (MDR) in Class IIa or Class I categories, indicating compliance with moderate-risk standards. A rapid increase in AI product development from 2017 to 2020, peaking in 2020 and followed by recent stabilization and saturation, was identified. In this work, the authors review the advancements in AI-based imaging applications, underscoring AI’s transformative potential for enhanced diagnostic support and focusing on the critical role of CNNs, regulatory challenges, and potential threats to human labor in the field of diagnostic imaging.

Abstract
--------

This comprehensive review unfolds a detailed narrative of Artificial Intelligence (AI) making its foray into radiology, a move that is catalysing transformational shifts in the healthcare landscape. It traces the evolution of radiology, from the initial discovery of X-rays to the application of machine learning and deep learning in modern medical image analysis. The primary focus of this review is to shed light on AI applications in radiology, elucidating their seminal roles in image segmentation, computer-aided diagnosis, predictive analytics, and workflow optimisation. A spotlight is cast on the profound impact of AI on diagnostic processes, personalised medicine, and clinical workflows, with empirical evidence derived from a series of case studies across multiple medical disciplines. However, the integration of AI in radiology is not devoid of challenges. The review ventures into the labyrinth of obstacles that are inherent to AI-driven radiology—data quality, the ’black box’ enigma, infrastructural and technical complexities, as well as ethical implications. Peering into the future, the review contends that the road ahead for AI in radiology is paved with promising opportunities. It advocates for continuous research, embracing avant-garde imaging technologies, and fostering robust collaborations between radiologists and AI developers. The conclusion underlines the role of AI as a catalyst for change in radiology, a stance that is firmly rooted in sustained innovation, dynamic partnerships, and a steadfast commitment to ethical responsibility.

**Keywords:** medical imaging, radiology, artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, convolutional neural networks, computer-aided diagnosis, radiomics

1. Introduction
---------------

Radiology, since its inception, has experienced a revolutionary journey, punctuating modern medicine with its profound influence. From the discovery of X-rays to the subsequent integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML), this multifaceted discipline continually evolves, transforming itself and the healthcare ecosystem it underpins.

This comprehensive review scrutinises the interplay of AI and ML in radiology, exploring their foundational principles, historical progression, practical applications, inherent challenges, and ethical dilemmas. By enriching understanding of AI and ML’s contributions to radiology, the review aims to foster insightful discussions among clinicians, researchers, and policymakers, ultimately shaping the field’s direction and enhancing patient outcomes. The exploration delves into the fundamental constructs of AI and ML, their growing influence in radiology, practical integration strategies, and illustrative case studies across various medical specialties. Further, it addresses challenges such as data quality, ethical concerns, and contemplates potential future directions in AI-driven radiology.

### 1.1. Radiology in Modern Medicine

Radiology, the medical discipline centred on the utilisation of imaging modalities to diagnose and treat diseases, has emerged as a cornerstone of contemporary medicine, forming an integral part of clinical practice. It extends beyond mere disease detection to encompass treatment guidance and ongoing disease management. Expertise in diagnostic modalities such as computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), ultrasound, and X-rays guide immediate clinical interventions, treatment monitoring, and chronicle a visual narrative of a patient’s health. The intricate insights into anatomical, physiological, and molecular disease processes provided by medical imaging have a significant impact on patient care, facilitating the tailoring of treatments, thereby improving therapeutic outcomes and minimising adverse effects [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B1-diagnostics-13-02760),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B2-diagnostics-13-02760),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B3-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Radiology serves as a crucial gear in the intricate machinery of interdisciplinary medical teams. Radiologists deliver precise, timely imaging reports, thus enhancing communication among various specialists and shaping crucial decisions, which contributes to a holistic, patient-focused healthcare approach [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B4-diagnostics-13-02760)]. As valued consultative partners, radiologists offer key insights into the choice and interpretation of suitable imaging studies, playing a significant role in radiation safety and dose management while their expertise elucidates the clinical picture, offering insights that can markedly influence patient management [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B5-diagnostics-13-02760),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B6-diagnostics-13-02760)].

### 1.2. From Roentgen to Magnetic Fields: A Brief History

The metamorphosis of modern medical imaging technology, from Wilhelm Roentgen’s pioneering discovery of X-ray technology in 1895 to contemporary advanced techniques, epitomises the relentless pursuit of scientific advancement and its profound impact on radiology ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#diagnostics-13-02760-f001)). Roentgen’s unprecedented X-ray innovation offered a non-invasive glimpse into the human body, forming the foundation for modern imaging. Despite its initial limitations in 2D representation and soft tissue contrast, this fundamental concept laid the groundwork for more sophisticated, non-invasive imaging modalities [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B7-diagnostics-13-02760)].

#### Figure 1.

[![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/df63/10487271/71bbc8920367/diagnostics-13-02760-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10487271_diagnostics-13-02760-g001.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/figure/diagnostics-13-02760-f001/)

A historical timeline of the key discoveries in medical imaging.

The advent of CT in 1973 by Sir Godfrey Hounsfield and Allan Cormack marked a significant milestone, transcending the limitations of 2D imaging by introducing a three-dimensional (3D) format [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B8-diagnostics-13-02760)]. CT utilises the basic principle of differential absorption but couples this with the synchronised rotation of X-ray sources and detectors around the patient’s body, paired with sophisticated computational algorithms, enabling the reconstruction of 3D volumetric data from the collected 2D images [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B7-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Introduced in the 20th century, ultrasound imaging marked a departure away from ionising radiation-based technologies by using high-frequency sound waves to create real-time images of internal body structures. Its non-ionising radiation nature, real-time imaging capability, and cost-effectiveness have allowed broad applicability in various clinical fields, such as obstetrics, gynaecology, cardiology, and emergency medicine [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B9-diagnostics-13-02760)]. As an indispensable tool in emergency and critical care medicine, its transformative role, especially through point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS), has facilitated rapid bedside assessments and hastened clinical decision-making [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B7-diagnostics-13-02760)].

In the 1970s, Paul Lauterbur and Sir Peter Mansfield led the development of MRI, a technology that employs a robust magnetic field and radio waves to generate exceptionally detailed images of the body, particularly of soft tissue structures [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B10-diagnostics-13-02760),[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B11-diagnostics-13-02760)]. The non-ionising nature of MRI, coupled with its unrivalled soft tissue differentiation ability, revolutionised medical imaging. Manipulation of RF pulse sequence timing in MRI further enhanced its diagnostic utility, enabling the acquisition of various image types and discernment of distinct tissues and pathologies [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B7-diagnostics-13-02760)].

### 1.3. From Film to Function: An Evolution in Radiology

Parallel to the disruptive innovations in imaging modalities, another pivotal shift was occurring in the late 20th century: the evolution from film-based to digital radiography and the introduction of Picture Archiving and Communication Systems (PACS). This transition radically improved the efficiency of image acquisition, storage, and retrieval, while also enabling the seamless sharing and transfer of images within and across healthcare institutions [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B12-diagnostics-13-02760)].

The transformation in medical imaging technology did not stop with these innovations. Functional imaging techniques such as PET, which is uniquely characterised by its use of radiolabelled biochemical substances, and Single-Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT), employing gamma-emitting radionuclides to trace biological processes, illuminated metabolic and biological processes, opening a window into cellular activity and providing invaluable insights into the functional status of organs [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B13-diagnostics-13-02760),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B14-diagnostics-13-02760)].

3D imaging heralded a significant evolution in medical imaging by providing a more precise understanding of spatial relationships within the body, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and surgical planning. The subsequent development of four-dimensional (4D) imaging further pushed the boundaries by incorporating the element of time, allowing for real-time monitoring of physiological processes [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B15-diagnostics-13-02760)].

The confluence of functional and anatomical imaging gave rise to hybrid imaging technologies such as PET/CT and SPECT/CT. These modalities amalgamate the strengths of both techniques, providing comprehensive diagnostic information. For instance, PET/CT combines the metabolic insight of PET with the detailed anatomical context of CT, considerably enhancing the accuracy of lesion localisation and characterisation [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B16-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Lastly, the emerging field of interventional radiology, which leverages imaging for guidance during minimally invasive procedures, has reshaped the healthcare landscape. By providing real-time visualisation of the target area, these procedures offer enhanced precision, potentially improving patient outcomes and reducing recovery times. For example, image-guided biopsies provide a safer and less invasive alternative to surgical biopsies, leading to fewer complications and shorter hospital stays [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B17-diagnostics-13-02760)].

### 1.4. A Glimpse into the Future: New Frontiers

Radiology’s future promises transformation through the integration of virtual/augmented reality (VR/AR) and AI, heralding a new era of medical imaging. Originating from the gaming and entertainment industries, VR/AR technologies are gradually permeating radiology, providing an immersive environment advantageous for radiology training and clinical practice. In the latter, the technologies can augment imaging data visualisation, thereby enhancing diagnosis and treatment planning [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B18-diagnostics-13-02760)].

AI, particularly its subset machine learning, is radically improving radiology, strengthening image analysis, and mitigating diagnostic errors. AI algorithms process and interpret data, performing tasks that emulate or even surpass human cognitive capabilities. ML, through exposure to labeled examples, is capable of extracting complex, high-level data, even from unlabelled datasets. By integrating AI into VR/AR technologies, the potential to boost radiological efficiency, improve diagnostic accuracy, and improve treatment planning exponentially exists [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B19-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Over the last two decades, the radiology community has refined computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) tools based on ML, which are set to bring about an integrated diagnostic service by incorporating radiology, pathology, and genomics data to improve the performance of CAD and enhance the productivity of radiology service by AI-assisted workflow [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B20-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Notwithstanding, the integration of AI and VR/AR in radiology encounters technical hurdles, notably incorporating AI-derived results into existing workflows. A proposed roadmap, however, advocates for AI-based image analysis algorithm integration, featuring a radiologist-AI feedback loop system for continuous improvement. This is exemplified by a case study demonstrating improved detection of brain metastases with AI application and radiologist feedback [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B21-diagnostics-13-02760)].

The ethical, legal, and societal implications of these technologies in radiology warrant careful scrutiny. The application of AI in radiology, while promising, can raise ethical issues, particularly related to bias and “black box” issues (i.e., the lack of transparency in AI decision-making processes). Advocacy for ethical AI underscores the need to address possible discriminatory effects and injustices, recommending future radiology-focused AI developments to incorporate social science perspectives [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B22-diagnostics-13-02760)].

2. The Fundamentals of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
-------------------------------------------------------------------

This section offers a journey through complex developments of AI and ML, sketching their historical trajectory and explaining the distinctive yet interconnected terminologies of AI, ML, and Deep Learning (DL). It also sheds light on the key ML algorithms and techniques that have shaped the technological landscape, underscoring the indelible imprint they have left on it.

### 2.1. Chronicle of Artificial Intelligence: Milestones and Breakthroughs

The compelling development of AI, spanning from antiquity—marked by folklore and tales of artificial beings—to our current era of sophisticated AI systems, has roots in the philosophical depictions of human cognition as a mechanistic process ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#diagnostics-13-02760-f002)). The inception of modern AI concepts is largely accredited to the development of the programmable digital computer in the 1940s, reaching a milestone with the field’s official establishment at the 1956 Dartmouth Conference [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B23-diagnostics-13-02760)].

#### Figure 2.

[![Image 10: Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/df63/10487271/107f9709b8a7/diagnostics-13-02760-g002.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10487271_diagnostics-13-02760-g002.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/figure/diagnostics-13-02760-f002/)

Significant milestones in the evolution of artificial intelligence.

The 1970s saw a significant leap forward with the emergence of rule-based expert systems like MYCIN, a seminal creation by Buchanan and Shortliffe [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B23-diagnostics-13-02760),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B24-diagnostics-13-02760)]. These expert systems, designed to replicate human expertise by leveraging knowledge bases and inference engines, laid the foundation for AI’s influential role in medical diagnosis and clinical decision-making.

Machine learning algorithms, which surfaced in subsequent years, spearheaded a novel paradigm for data-driven predictions and classification. Decision trees introduced in 1986, support vector machines in 1995, and neural networks in 1986, collectively broadened the AI horizon in healthcare [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B25-diagnostics-13-02760),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B26-diagnostics-13-02760),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B27-diagnostics-13-02760)]. These algorithms propelled the analysis of extensive datasets, thereby inaugurating a novel epoch of pattern recognition and predictive modelling in healthcare.

The turn of the century witnessed a paradigm shift with the advent of deep learning approaches, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs). Surpassing previous methodologies in image recognition tasks, CNNs, architecturally modelled on structure and function of the human brain, precipitated major advancements in medical image classification, segmentation, and detection, due to their capacity to learn hierarchical representations from vast amounts of labelled data [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B28-diagnostics-13-02760),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B29-diagnostics-13-02760)].

The accelerated progression of AI in recent years is a by-product of the synergistic interplay of two factors: the surge in big data and advancements in computational power. The widespread dissemination of electronic health records (EHRs), medical imaging archives, and annotated datasets provide ample training data, while progress in hardware, including graphical processing units (GPUs) and distributed computing, have expedited the deployment of computationally demanding AI algorithms [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B30-diagnostics-13-02760)].

The rapid advancement of AI language models, such as GPT-4, has led to unique applications and implications across a multitude of discplines, including healthcare and medical imaging [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B31-diagnostics-13-02760)]. These models, whilst capable of generating human-like text and facilitating communication, have raised salient concerns. The potential for these models to contribute substantially to medical research and patient care is undeniable; however, experts express reservations about their limitations and potential to inadvertently engender inequities or disseminate misinformation, thus emphasising the need for robust strategies to manage these risks responsibly. These strategies include increasing transparency about potential harms, promoting early detection of issues, and implementing regulatory measures and peer reviews [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B32-diagnostics-13-02760)]. By doing so, the goal is to ensure that AI technologies, including language models, are utilised optimally and ethically, contributing positively to medical imaging and healthcare outcomes.

By recognising these milestones, we can appreciate the progression of AI in healthcare, particularly in medical imaging. The intertwining of rule-based systems, traditional machine learning algorithms, and the transformative influence of deep learning techniques has laid the foundation for the current state-of-the-art AI applications in radiology and other medical specialties.

### 2.2. Decoding the Terminology: AI, ML, and DL

The computational intelligence landscape is an expansive system, constituted of distinct yet interconnected systems, with each sector playing a specific role and interacts uniquely within the data science sphere ([Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#diagnostics-13-02760-f003)).

Artificial intelligence is defined as the replication of human intelligence in machines that are programmed to emulate human cognition and actions, encompassing learning, problem-solving, reasoning, and perception. AI can be classified into two major types: narrow AI, which is designed for specific tasks (e.g., facial recognition or voice commands), and general AI, which mimics a broader spectrum of human intellect. AI aims to develop systems with autonomous intelligent functionality, capable of problem-solving, decision-making, and performing tasks typically requiring human intelligence [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B33-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Machine learning, a subset of AI, is centred around the development of software that learns autonomously from accessed data. The learning process is derived from the analysis of observations or data to identify patterns and make informed future decisions based on these observations. Its fundamental objective is to enable computers to adapt their actions autonomously without human intervention. The primary categories of ML algorithms are supervised learning, which predicts or classifies new data based on examples, and unsupervised learning, which identifies inherent patterns and structures within the data without guidance from pre-established outputs [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B34-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Deep learning, an ML subset, utilises multilayered artificial neural networks (aptly coined “deep”), enabling DL algorithms to model and understand complex data patterns. These algorithms are especially effective for tasks where manual feature extraction proves challenging, such as image or speech recognition. It is noteworthy that these feature layers are data-learned, not human-engineered, embodying an architecture inspired by the human brain and have proven successful in visual recognition tasks, even outperforming human performance [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B28-diagnostics-13-02760)].

Recognising the nuanced interrelationships between AI, ML, and DL aids in conceptualising each subfield’s contribution and progression within the broader AI narrative. AI lays the foundation for ML and DL. ML amplifies AI’s potential by enabling machine data learning, and DL further deepens these capabilities with neural networks that decipher complex data patterns. Each field enriches the wider AI domain, culminating in the modern AI landscape where each layer contributes to the evolution of intelligent systems.

### 2.3. Machine Learning Foundations: Algorithms and Techniques

Machine learning is anchored by a plethora of algorithms and techniques that enable computers to learn from data. The two primary types of machine learning, supervised and unsupervised learning, lie at the heart of this field.

Supervised learning, a dominant machine learning variant, harnesses pre-established examples or training data, consisting of input-output pairs. The objective is to formulate a function that maps input data to corresponding outputs, enabling accurate predictions or classifications for unfamiliar data. Central to supervised learning are algorithms such as linear regression, logistic regression, and decision trees [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B36-diagnostics-13-02760)].

In contrast, unsupervised learning navigates the data space autonomously to uncover inherent patterns, structures, or relationships devoid of predefined outputs. Its focus is on discovering intrinsic data structures, thus offering insights that could potentially solve complex problems. Notable unsupervised learning algorithms include clustering techniques like k-means, hierarchical clustering, and dimensionality reduction methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10487271/#B37-diagnostics-13-02760)].
